coredata.dc:title: Resonant energy transfer between plasmonic silver and biomolecule for colour tuning and white light emission
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: John, Jancy
Abraham, Rani
Jayakrishnan, R.
Thomas, Vinoy
coredata.dc:description: A simple eco-friendly method was employed to generate white light using pomegranate-PVA - curcumin mediated silver nanoparticles (CAg NP-PVA) mixture. The white light emission is obtained by integrating the green emitting curcumin and blue emitting pomegranate extract. Controlling the emission of curcumin with Ag NP-PVA and mixing with pomegranate extract resulted in efficient white light with the Commission Internationale d’Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinate (0.28,0.31). Ag NP-PVA and anthocyanin played a significant role in obtaining CIE coordinate close to that of pure white light (0.33,0.33). The optimized white light emission obtained from pomegranate- CAg NP-PVA mixture is cheap and fairly green in nature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: White light emission
Anthocyanins
Silver nanoparticles
Curcumin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption and Emission spectra of CAg NP-PVA.
Photograph of curcumin under daylight and UV excitation.
Absorption and emission spectra of Pom extract.
Photograph of Pom extract under daylight and UV excitation.

Chromaticity plot for colour coordinates of Pom extract (0.18,0.08) and CAg NP (0.26,0.65). . (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Change in the emission spectra of Pom extract with the addition of curcumin.
CIE plot for pom extract (P) and curcumin (C).
Change in the emission spectra of Pom extract with the addition of CAg NP.
CIE plot of Pom- CAg NP.
Change in the emission spectra of Pom extract with the addition of CAg NP-PVA.
CIE plot of pom- CAg NP-PVA.
TEM image of white light emitting mixture obtained from Pom-CAg NP-PVA.
White light emission generated from the combination of Pom extract and curcumin.
Variation of chromaticity coordinates obtained from Pom-CAg NP.
Variation of colour coordinates from the combination of Pom and CAgNP-PVA.
Chromaticity coordinate obtained from three different combinations.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Dr Jancy John reports financial support was provided by UGC MANF. Dr Vinoy Thomas reports financial support was provided by SERB.

head.ce:title: Resonant energy transfer between plasmonic silver and biomolecule for colour tuning and white light emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Christian College, University of Kerala, 689122, Chengannur, Trivandrum, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Christian College-689122, Chengannur, University of Kerala, Trivandrum, Kerala, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Christian College, University of Kerala, 689122, Chengannur, Trivandrum, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Christian College-689122, Chengannur, University of Kerala, Trivandrum, Kerala, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Kariavattom, 695581, Trivandrum, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Kariavattom-695581, Trivandrum, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple eco-friendly method was employed to generate white light using pomegranate-PVA - curcumin mediated silver nanoparticles (CAg NP-PVA) mixture. The white light emission is obtained by integrating the green emitting curcumin and blue emitting pomegranate extract. Controlling the emission of curcumin with Ag NP-PVA and mixing with pomegranate extract resulted in efficient white light with the Commission Internationale d’Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinate (0.28,0.31). Ag NP-PVA and anthocyanin played a significant role in obtaining CIE coordinate close to that of pure white light (0.33,0.33). The optimized white light emission obtained from pomegranate- CAg NP-PVA mixture is cheap and fairly green in nature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Colour tunable white light emitting materials have caught the attention of researchers on account of their possible applications in light emitting devices, sensors, displays, artificial lighting, imaging etc [1–8]. White light is usually generated by a combination of materials and the white light emitting systems currently known are composed of materials like quantum dots, nanomaterials, polymers, rare earth doped materials, metal-organic frameworks and inorganic-organic hybrids. However, the presence of toxic chemicals in these materials is a drawback which restricts their use in various applications [1,3,5,6,9]. This situation warrants the use of eco-friendly non-toxic fluorescent materials which brings natural plant dyes into the frame due to their non-toxic nature, low cost, intense fluorescence emission and ecological compatibility [1].

Natural plant dyes are primarily obtained from the extracts of various parts like stem, root, leaf, fruits etc. of the plant. The extracts contain a wide range of phytochemicals whose structure and composition varies depending on the plant and the part extracted. For instance, extract of pomegranate is rich in anthocyanins and polyphenolic compounds while that of turmeric is rich in the phenolic curcuminoid, curcumin. Singh and Mishra reported the production of white light emission (WLE) using pomegranate (Pom) and turmeric in aqueous ethanol, gelatin gel and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film [2]. They have also reported the white light emission from a mixture of Pom, turmeric and spinach extracts [10] and from Pom seeds and carbon nanoparticles as well [11]. Mathew et al. demonstrated WLE from arecanut extract and Carbon nanoparticles obtained from arecanut [12].

In the present investigation we have focused on producing white light using pom extract-curcumin; pom-curcumin mediated Ag NPs (Pom-CAg NP) and pom-PVA doped with CAg NP (Pom-CAg NP-PVA). Pom extract shows two emissions one at 420 ​nm which corresponds to the polyphenols and the other around 600 ​nm due to anthocyanins. An eco-friendly, safe method was chosen to produce Ag NPs using Curcumin which role-played as reducing agent and stabilizing component too. The study thus reports the tuning of white light emission using nanoparticles besides its production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PVA doped with silver nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin solution was prepared by dissolving 2 ​mg of curcumin powder in ethanol. 5 ​ml of this solution was mixed with 10 ​ml of 1 ​mM AgNO3 solution. The solution was stirred at 600 ​rpm for 1 ​h during which the colour of the solution changed from pale yellow to yellow indicating the formation of Ag NPs. 5 ​wt % of PVA solution was then mixed with this curcumin mediated Ag NPs (CAg NPs) and stirred continuously for 30 ​min.

Fresh pomegranate (Pom) fruits were collected from Chengannur, Kerala, India. Red seeds of fresh Pom were squeezed and centrifuged for 30 ​min at 5000 ​rpm. The supernatant solution was then filtered. It was reported that this solution contains polyphenols and anthocyanin as main components [2].

White light emission was achieved using three different sample mixtures, viz., pom extract-curcumin, pom extract-CAg NP and Pom extract-CAg NP-PVA mixture. Chromaticity coordinates were determined for the three sample mixtures (Pom-Curcumin, Pom-CAg NP, Pom-CAgNP-PVA).

UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with Shimadzu UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Model UV 1800) and emission studies with Jasco Spectrofluorometer (Model FP 8500). Transmission Electron Microscopic (TEM) images were recorded with JEM JEOL Transmission Electron Microscope and CIE colour coordinates estimated using Osram Sylvania software.
UV–Vis absorption spectrum of curcumin is shown in Fig. 1 . A sharp absorption occurs at 415 ​nm which is reported to be due to the diaryl heptanoid chromophore group of curcumin [13].

Fig. 2 shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum and emission spectrum of Ag NP-PVA synthesized using curcumin. An absorption peak at 436 ​nm is primarily due to the presence of plasmonic Ag NPs. Curcumin shows an emission peak at 511 ​nm. An enhancement in the fluorescence of curcumin is due to the presence of Ag NPs. Digital images of CAg NPs under daylight and UV light are shown in Fig. 3 .

Absorption and emission spectrum of pom extract is shown in Fig. 4 . Peak at 530 ​nm in the absorption spectrum is due to anthocyanins. It was reported that anthocyanins generally display weak fluorescence. Pom extract show two bands one at 440 ​nm which is due to polyphenols and the other around 600 ​nm which is due to the presence of anthocyanin. Photograph of Pom extract under daylight and UV excitation are shown in Fig. 5 .

Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) plot is a colour space model developed by the International Commission on IIlumination. It is a mapping software that make use of the three colour combinations: Red, Green and Blue and the corresponding colour is plotted in a three dimensional (3D) space. CIE diagram is usually plotted from the fluorescence data and is represented by two coordinates x and y. Chromaticity colour coordinates were determined using Osram Sylvania software [14]. CIE chromaticity coordinates were calculated from the spectral power distribution of the light source and the CIE colour matching functions. Colour functions
x ¯
,
y ¯
,
z ¯
gives the tristimulus value X, Y and Z [15]
X = ∫ p
x ¯
d λ , Y = ∫ p
y ¯
d λ
a n d
Z = ∫ p
z ¯
d λ
Where p is the SPD of the light source.

From X, Y and Z, the chromaticity coordinates were obtained as follows [16]
x =
X
X + Y + Z
y =
Y
X + Y + Z

For Pom extract, CIE coordinate appear in the blue region with coordinate (0.18,0.08) and the chromaticity of CAg NP in the CIE plot appears in the green region and the corresponding colour coordinate is (0.26, 0.65). Fig. 6 shows the chromaticity plot of CAg NP and Pom extract. The points A and B in the CIE plot lie on both side of the white region. This helps in producing the white light emission using pom extract and CAg NPs.

The path of colour coordinate passes very near to the pure white coordinate. White light emission spectrum covered the complete visible region from 400 ​nm to 700 ​nm with emission peaks at 420 ​nm, 490 ​nm and a broadband around 600 ​nm. Changes in the Pom and Pom-curcumin composition resulted in an interesting trajectory in the CIE plot (Fig. 8 ). Spectral changes from curcumin to Pom can be attributed to Förster Resonant Energy Transfer (FRET).

Peaks at 410 ​nm, 500 ​nm and a broad band ranging from 550 ​nm to 650 ​nm in the emission spectrum corresponds to the polyphenols, curcumin and anthocyanins respectively. Solution shows chromaticity coordinate (0.28,0.31) which is relatively closer to pure white coordinate (0.33,0.33) (Fig. 12 ). To monitor the stability of the WLE mixture, the samples were observed periodically. It was found that the WLE Pom-CAg NP-PVA sample mixture was stable for 3 months.

TEM image of white light emitting mixture obtained from Pom-CAg NP-PVA is shown in Fig. 13 . TEM image reveals the presence of Ag NPs in the white light emitting mixture of Pom-CAg NP-PVA. Particles are almost spherical with an average size of around 25 ​nm.

The studies thus revealed white light emission from three different combinations viz., Pom-Curcumin, Pom-C Ag NP and Pom-C Ag NP-PVA. The obtained CIE coordinates of three mixtures are listed in Table 4 .

CIE coordinate obtained from Pom-Curcumin, Pom-CAg NP and Pom-CAg NP-PVA are (0.24,0.29), (0.25,0.30) and (0.28, 0.31) respectively. The chromaticity coordinate of white light emitting mixture obtained from Pom-CAg NP-PVA is (0.28, 0.31) is close to pure white light emission (0.33,0.33). Thus Pom-CAg NP-PVA mixture appears to be an eco-friendly material for generation of white light.

A simple, green and cost-effective method was used to bring about white light emission from natural dyes and Ag NP. Optical absorption studies and fluorescence studies revealed the ability of three combinations to produce white light: Pom-Curcumin, Pom-CAg NP and Pom-CAgNP-PVA. White light emission with CIE coordinates (0.28,0.31) from Pom-C Ag NP-PVA mixture was found to be closer to the standard white light coordinates which makes the system a potential eco-friendly candidate in the field of white light emission.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author Jancy John is thankful to UGC (Govt. of India) for the financial support. Vinoy Thomas is thankful to SERB (Government of India) for financial assistance in the form of a research grant (EMR/2017/000178). The financial assistance in the form of DBT-Star scheme and Kerala State council for Science Technology and Environment (Student project) are also gratefully acknowledged.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White light emission from a single plant source extract with tunable photoluminescence
Spectrochim. Acta Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shi, Qiang, Shengxiang Xu, Bing Yang, Susu Duan, Shuhong Li, Dong Zhang, Qingru Wang, Ling Zhao, and Wenjun Wang. "White light emission from a single plant source extract with tunable photoluminescence." Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy 236 (2020): 118352 1-7.

Sci. Rep.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Singh, Vikram, and Ashok K. Mishra. "White light emission from vegetable extracts." Scientific reports 5, no. 1 (2015): 1-9.

Chem.--Eur. J.
